questions.question: What does each 2-point increase in the MEDAS (Mediterranean Diet Adherence Score) predict?

options.text: A specific number of pounds lost
Measurable improvements in inflammatory markers and mental health outcomes
The need for fewer vitamins and supplements
Better athletic performance

questions.explanation: Research shows that each 2-point increase in MEDAS score is associated with measurable improvements in inflammatory markers and mental health outcomes. You do not need to reach a perfect score of 14 — moving from 4 to 6 is just as meaningful as moving from 10 to 12. The dose-response relationship means progress at any level counts.

questions.question: If your estimated UPF (ultra-processed food) intake is 50% or higher, what does the research suggest about your mental health risk?

options.text: You are in the range associated with 53% higher odds of common mental disorders
There is no significant risk at any UPF level
You are at average risk, which is considered safe
Risk only applies to people who eat 80% or more UPF

questions.explanation: A UPF intake of 50% or higher puts you at or above the Western average and in the range associated with 53% higher odds of common mental disorders (anxiety and depression). The dose-response relationship means every 10% reduction is associated with a measurable decrease in depression risk.

questions.question: In the simplified Inflammatory Balance assessment, what does a negative net score indicate?

options.text: A healthy, anti-inflammatory dietary pattern
That you are eating fewer calories than needed
A pro-inflammatory dietary pattern with significant room for improvement
That the assessment is invalid

questions.explanation: In the Inflammatory Balance assessment, the net score equals your anti-inflammatory total minus your pro-inflammatory total. A negative score (-1 to -10) means pro-inflammatory foods outnumber anti-inflammatory ones in your regular diet, indicating significant opportunity for improvement. The priority should be adding anti-inflammatory foods rather than only restricting pro-inflammatory ones.

questions.question: How often should you use dietary self-assessment tools like the MEDAS to track your dietary pattern?

options.text: Daily — to catch every deviation from your plan
Monthly — enough to track trends without triggering food anxiety
Once a year at your annual physical
Only when you feel like your diet has gotten worse

questions.explanation: Monthly scoring is recommended — enough to track trends and reveal actionable patterns without triggering food anxiety or obsessive tracking. Research shows that individuals who regularly self-assess their dietary patterns are more likely to make and sustain positive changes. The key is using tools periodically, not obsessively.

questions.question: What guiding principle should you use when choosing which dietary changes to make based on your assessment results?

options.text: Fix everything at once for maximum impact
Focus on the most difficult changes first to build discipline
Highest impact, lowest effort — target changes that move your scores the most for the least disruption
Only make changes that involve buying new supplements

questions.explanation: The guiding principle is 'highest impact, lowest effort' — start with changes that will move your scores the most for the least disruption to your daily life. For example, switching cooking oil to olive oil is a single change with multiple benefits, and replacing your most frequent UPF with a whole-food alternative can shift your UPF percentage significantly.

title: Practical Assessment: Your Dietary Pattern Profile - Quiz
